title: Tight back vs hollow back, two spine constructions
subtitle: How the spine is glued determines how the book opens and ages
excerpt: A tight-back spine is glued directly to the leather or cloth covering material; the cover bends as the book opens. A hollow-back spine has a paper tube that lets the covering material stay still while the text block opens. Both have their place, the choice affects repairability, opening angle, and longevity.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on forwarding and finishing. Cockerell distinguishes the two spine types and recommends the tight back for fine work. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

glossaryTerms.definition: A spine where the covering material is glued directly to the mull-lined spine of the text block. The cover flexes with the book as it opens.
A spine where a paper tube is glued to the spine and the covering material is glued to the outside of the tube. The cover stays still while the text block opens inside.
The channel between the book's spine and the board, formed at the joint during forwarding. The board hinges in this groove when the book opens.

content.text: Open a well-made hardback and watch the spine. If the covering material curves tightly with the body of the book, it is a
tight back
. If the spine stays almost flat while the text block arcs forward, it is a
hollow back
. The difference is not visible from the outside, it is in the construction layer between the spine and the cloth.
The tight back

In a tight-back construction, the mull, super, and kraft-paper lining are glued to the rounded and backed spine of the text block. The cloth or leather covering material is then glued directly to that lined spine, there is no gap between spine and cover.

When the book opens, the covering material bends. This works well for flexible covering materials such as leather, which can absorb repeated flexing, but causes cracking along the spine of cloth-covered books over decades of heavy use. The reward is a firm, controlled opening action and a spine that presses cleanly flat when the book is closed.
The tight back is Cockerell's preference for fine bindings. It demands a well-rounded spine and careful covering, any unevenness in the lining reads through the covering material.
The hollow back

A hollow back inserts a paper tube between spine and cover. The tube is made by folding a strip of kraft paper into two or three layers, gluing the layers together, and gluing the assembly to the spine lining. The covering material is glued to the outside of the tube.

When the book opens, the tube concertinas slightly while the cover stays mostly flat. The covering material is not stressed, which makes the hollow back far more resistant to spine-crack failure on cloth bindings. Most modern publisher's bindings are hollow-back variants for exactly this reason.

The drawback is feel: a hollow-back spine has a slight springiness when pressed, and the book does not close with quite the same solid authority as a tight back. The tube must be fitted precisely to the spine width or the book will open unevenly.
The French groove
Both constructions rely on the
French groove

, the channel along each side of the spine at the joint. The board sits in this groove and hinges there when the book opens. A well-formed groove means the board opens cleanly and does not stress the joint; a shallow or absent groove causes the board to hinge at the wrong point, cracking the covering material at the joint within a year.
Which to choose

For a leather-covered fine binding that will be used occasionally: tight back. For a cloth-covered journal that will be opened every day: hollow back. For a guest book or display piece: tight back. For a working reference book or notebook: hollow back.
For cloth-covered books, the hollow back is more forgiving. A small covering imperfection hides in the tube gap rather than showing through the cloth.
